course: ML 2023 (Hung-yi Lee) — Prompting / How to Harness Large Language Models (Finetune vs. Prompt)

candidates.why_it_matters: Core mechanism behind treating an LLM as a generalist: the model performs a new task from a few in-prompt examples without weight updates. The lecture devotes ~15 slides to it, including the surprising 'Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations' finding that label correctness matters less than the label space and input/format distribution.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 20-34: machine learns to read task examples. Sentiment-analysis demonstrations are shown; experiments deliberately use wrong labels and irrelevant inputs (arxiv 2202.12837) showing performance barely drops — the demonstrations mostly tell the model the label space and that it should do that task, rather than teaching it from scratch. Later work (2303.03846) shows larger models can actually learn from flipped labels.

candidates.concept: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and its variants (zero-shot CoT, self-consistency, least-to-most)
candidates.why_it_matters: CoT dramatically improves multi-step reasoning (arithmetic, word problems) by asking the model to produce intermediate reasoning steps. Variants — zero-shot 'Let's think step by step', self-consistency (sample multiple chains and majority-vote), and least-to-most decomposition — are practical, widely used prompting techniques students can directly apply.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 41-47: CoT prompting (2201.11903) adds reasoning steps to demonstrations; zero-shot CoT (2205.11916) just appends 'think step by step'; self-consistency (2203.11171) samples several reasoning paths and votes (chicken-rabbit example: 5 sampled answers, majority correct); least-to-most prompting (2205.10625) decomposes a hard problem into sub-problems.

candidates.concept: Specialist vs. Generalist: Finetune vs. Prompt as two ways to use an LLM
candidates.why_it_matters: The organizing framework of the whole lecture: expectation one (a specialist that beats generalists on a single task via finetuning) vs. expectation two (a generalist steered purely by prompts, the ChatGPT route). Understanding this trade-off frames every other technique in the course.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 1-11: two expectations for LLMs — become a specialist (solve one task, can win on that single task, e.g. ChatGPT-as-translator studies 2301.08745 / 2302.09210) vs. become a generalist (do everything, just redesign the prompt, no coding). These map to two usage paradigms.

candidates.why_it_matters: Explains how a generalist model is trained to follow natural-language instructions across many tasks (FLAN), enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen instructions — the training-side counterpart to prompting that underlies modern instruction-following models.
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 35-40: instruction-tuning trains on (instruction, input, output) triples over many tasks (translate/summarize/edit) and tests on held-out instructions; FLAN (Finetuned Language Net, 2109.01652) and 2110.08207 cited as key examples.

candidates.concept: Adapting pretrained models: Head, Finetune, and Adapter (parameter-efficient finetuning)
candidates.why_it_matters: Shows the specialist route in detail: add a task head, finetune all weights, or insert small Adapter modules so you store one big model plus many tiny adapters instead of 100 full copies — the foundational idea behind today's parameter-efficient finetuning (PEFT/LoRA).
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 12-18: reshape a pretrained model by adding a head, full finetuning, or Adapters; with adapters you keep 1 large model + 100 small adapters instead of 100 large models (adapter survey 2210.06175).

candidates.concept: Automatic prompt search: Hard prompt vs. Soft prompt (RL-based and LM-based, e.g. APE)
candidates.why_it_matters: Frames prompts as something that can be optimized rather than hand-written: hard prompts (discrete tokens, optionally found via RL) vs. soft prompts (trainable continuous embeddings, an 'adapter at the input'), and using an LLM itself to generate prompts (Automatic Prompt Engineer).
candidates.transcript_summary: Slides 48-52: hard vs. soft prompt; soft prompt is a trainable input embedding learned from task-specific data; RL-based prompt search (2206.03931) and using an LM to generate prompts / APE (2211.01910) which can rediscover instructions like 'decide if this sentence is positive or negative'.

selected_concepts: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and its variants (zero-shot CoT, self-consistency, least-to-most)
Specialist vs. Generalist: Finetune vs. Prompt as two ways to use an LLM
selection_rationale: Selected for the best joint score on importance and demo feasibility while covering the lecture's spine. In-context Learning is the central concept and is highly demoable: a small notebook can build few-shot prompts, then reproduce the 'wrong labels / irrelevant inputs' ablation to show that the label space and format — not example correctness — drive most of the effect. Chain-of-Thought is the lecture's most practically actionable technique and is trivially interactive (compare direct answer vs. step-by-step vs. self-consistency majority vote on arithmetic/word problems). Specialist-vs-Generalist (Finetune vs. Prompt) is the framing that makes the other two coherent and can be illustrated with a lightweight conceptual plus zero-shot-vs-few-shot comparison. Instruction tuning, Adapters, and automatic prompt search are important but require heavier training infrastructure or model access, lowering demo feasibility for a classroom Colab; they remain strong follow-up candidates.
assumptions: Notebook will run in Google Colab (Python 3.12) and should prefer small/hosted models or a configurable LLM API so demos stay executable without large GPUs.
Demos should degrade gracefully (e.g., use a small open model like GPT-2 / Flan-T5-small, or mock/cached responses) when no API key is available, to preserve executability — the top quality priority.
Source is lecture slides (Traditional Chinese with English terms); no instructor transcript was provided, so transcript_summary fields are derived from slide content and cited papers.
Reproducing the exact 'Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations' numbers is out of scope; the demo will illustrate the qualitative phenomenon on a small scale.
